tossups.question: This author advocated “[worshipping] Darwin and Ibsen” and praised Ibsen’s “all or nothing” approach in a “random thought” later collected in the book Jottings Under Lamplight. A speech by this author discussed the “compulsion” of a character for whom “Dreams are fine, but otherwise money is essential.” In a preface, this writer described abandoning medical school to treat the spirit of his country’s people, who are imagined as sleepers fated to painlessly suffocate within an (*) “iron house without windows.” This writer coined the “gods and demons” genre to describe fantasy novels like The Investiture of the Gods in a 1930 “Brief History” of his country’s fiction. This author, who lectured at a women’s college on “What Happens After Nora Leaves Home?”, wrote in vernacular for a collection of stories aligned with the May Fourth movement. For 10 points, name this Chinese critic whose collection Call to Arms contains “A Madman’s Diary.”
tossups.answer: Lu Xun [or Lu Hsün; or Zhou Shuren] (The third sentence describes the preface to Call to Arms.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Lu Xun [or Lu Hsun; or Zhou Shuren] (The third sentence describes the preface to Call to Arms.)
tossups.question_sanitized: This author advocated "[worshipping] Darwin and Ibsen" and praised Ibsen's "all or nothing" approach in a "random thought" later collected in the book Jottings Under Lamplight. This writer coined the "gods and demons" genre to describe fantasy novels like The Investiture of the Gods in a 1930 "Brief History" of his country's fiction. This author, who lectured at a women's college on "What Happens After Nora Leaves Home?", wrote in vernacular for a collection of stories aligned with the May Fourth movement. For 10 points, name this Chinese critic whose collection Call to Arms contains "A Madman's Diary."

bonuses.leadin: The trilogy consisting of Stories to Enlighten the World, Stories to Caution the World, and Stories to Awaken the World was written in this linguistic variety. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this linguistic variety used to write works in the huaben genre, such as the 17th-century collection Slapping the Table in Amazement.
The use of written vernacular Chinese was promoted by the May Fourth Movement, which included the short stories of this author of “The True Story of Ah Q” and “A Madman’s Diary.”
The aforementioned stories appeared in this 1922 collection by Lu Xun, whose preface includes a memorable metaphor about waking sleepers who are about to suffocate in an indestructible iron house.
answers: vernacular Chinese [or baihua; or vernacular Mandarin; prompt on “Chinese” or “Mandarin”; do NOT accept or prompt on “classical Chinese”]
answers_sanitized: vernacular Chinese [or baihua; or vernacular Mandarin; prompt on "Chinese" or "Mandarin"; do NOT accept or prompt on "classical Chinese"]
Lu Xun [or Zhou Shuren; or Lu Hsun]
A Call to Arms [or Nahan; or Outcry]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: The trilogy consisting of Stories to Enlighten the World, Stories to Caution the World, and Stories to Awaken the World was written in this linguistic variety. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this linguistic variety used to write works in the huaben genre, such as the 17th-century collection Slapping the Table in Amazement.
The use of written vernacular Chinese was promoted by the May Fourth Movement, which included the short stories of this author of "The True Story of Ah Q" and "A Madman's Diary."
The aforementioned stories appeared in this 1922 collection by Lu Xun, whose preface includes a memorable metaphor about waking sleepers who are about to suffocate in an indestructible iron house.

bonuses.leadin: In the often-printed preface to this work, the author claims he's been unable to erase these stories from his memory, and relates his youthful travels to a pawnbroker and a medicine shop. FTPE:
parts: Name this 1922 short story collection which contains "A Madman's Diary," and ”The True Story of Ah Q.” It was the first collection by its author, released before Wandering.
Of course, all of those works were written by this 20th century Chinese author who also wrote "Storm in a Teacup."

First published in New Youth magazine, this story in Call to Arms describes a title scholar who is impoverished because he cannot pass the Imperial examination, and resorts to thievery while copying manuscripts.
answers_sanitized: Call to Arms [or Na Han or Cry Out]
